# Introduction

## Defining Discrimination and Fairness

When we talk about discrimination, we usually refer to the "Treatment or
consideration based on class or category, such as race or gender, rather than
individual merit; partiality or prejudice" [@americanheritage], which inherently
carries a negative connotation. However, the term can also carry a more neutral
meaning: "the act, practice, or an instance of separating or distinguishing
categorically rather than individually" [@merriamwebster].

In the insurance context, the term "discrimination", or sometimes referred to as
risk classification, is the practice by which insurers categorize policyholders
into distinct risk pools based on observable differences in their risk profiles,
in order to charge premiums that reflect each group's level of risk. Insurance
discrimination serves two primary economic justifications: it enables accurate
pricing by aligning premiums with actual risk levels, and it incentivizes
insureds to adopt risk-reducing behaviors in exchange for lower premiums.

However, what is unique in insurance is that even statistical discrimination,
which by definition is absent of any malicious intent, can pose moral and legal
challenges [@avraham2017].

Following @freeshwang2021, we use the term in a neutral manner.

## Regulatory Landscape

As a well-known example of antidiscrimination legislation, the EU "Gender
Directive" [@genderdirective] serves as the prime example of the prohibition of
the use of a protected variable.

In the U.S., insurers can be restricted in the use of a protected variable. If
this variable is an important rating factor and allowed to be used, regulators
can limit its impact on compressing total premium ranges between the high-risk
group and the low-risk group. [Certifying and Removing Disparate Impact]

## The Pillars of Fairness




### Actuarial Fairness

### Group Fairness

### Individual Fairness

## Mitigation and Testing

Mitigating discrimination is usually seen as paradoxical, because in order to
avoid discrimination, we must create another discrimination — the point of the
"reverse discrimination objection".

### Pre-processing

### In-processing

### Post-processing

## Two Worldviews

See @fig-effort.

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\IfFileExists{img/effort.png}{\includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{img/effort.png}}{\fbox{\rule{0pt}{4cm}\rule{0.6\textwidth}{0pt} placeholder figure}}
\caption{Une figure}
\label{fig-effort}
\end{figure}
```


## 1.3 Related work
 
Our contribution sits at the intersection of two literatures that have so far developed largely in parallel.
 
**Multi-peril and dependent-risk pricing.** The idea of modelling insurance coverages jointly
rather than in isolation is most closely associated with Frees and co-authors. Frees et al.
(2009) introduced a hierarchical claims model separating occurrence and severity with dependence
components; Frees et al. (2010) proposed dependent multi-peril ratemaking models under the
individual risk model, using multivariate binary regression and Gaussian copulas to capture
dependence in claim occurrence across coverages; and Frees et al. (2016) extended this to the
collective risk model, modelling multivariate frequencies and severities with copulas. Related
strands use copula-based multivariate Tweedie models (Shi, 2016), longitudinal multi-peril
ratemaking for property insurance (Yang and Shi, 2019), multilevel copula models (Shi et al.,
2016), and cross-product credibility that links, for example, home and motor insurance at the
household level (Pechon et al., 2021). Across this body of work the recurring message is that
perils are dependent and that jointly modelling them improves both experience rating and risk
margins relative to the univariate convention. None of these works, however, considers fairness
or the presence of protected attributes.
 
**Fairness in pricing and optimal-transport methods.** Within actuarial science, Lindholm et
al. (2022) introduced *discrimination-free* pricing, a procedure that first fits a model on all
information and then averages out the direct effect of the protected attribute; subsequent work
clarified the relationship between proxy discrimination and demographic disparities and the
attendant loss of the balance property (Lindholm et al., 2024). A complementary and, for our
purposes, more directly relevant line uses **optimal transport** to enforce demographic parity.
In the machine-learning literature, Gordaliza et al. (2019) used optimal transport to repair
data, and Chzhen et al. (2020) and Le Gouic et al. (2020) established the key characterization
that the demographic-parity-optimal predictor under squared loss is obtained by pushing each
group-conditional score distribution onto their **Wasserstein-2 barycenter**. Charpentier, Hu
and Ratz (2023) brought this technique into insurance, replacing the crude re-scaling of premiums
with a Wasserstein-barycenter post-processing step that achieves strong demographic parity while
minimizing distortion of the original tariff. The multi-task extension of Hu et al. (2023), which
connects fairness across several simultaneous tasks to *multi-marginal* Wasserstein barycenters,
is the closest existing work to the multi-peril setting, and recent work on
sliced-Wasserstein barycenters for *marginal* fairness (Nguyen and Ho, 2024) points toward
scalable solutions in higher dimensions. What is missing is a treatment tailored to the
multi-peril actuarial problem, where the object to be made fair is a dependent vector of
coverage premiums and where the intervention must be both theoretically grounded and
computationally tractable at portfolio scale.
 
## 1.4 Contributions
 
This thesis develops a **post-processing framework** for mitigating demographic-parity
unfairness in multi-peril nonlife insurance pricing based on Wasserstein and sliced Wasserstein
barycenters. Post-processing is deliberate: it acts on the outputs of any already-fitted pricing
model, whatever its functional form, and therefore integrates with existing actuarial pipelines
without requiring them to be re-estimated under fairness constraints.
 
The starting point is the observation that the single-peril case is, in a sense, already solved.
When the premium is a scalar, the Wasserstein-2 barycenter of the group-conditional premium
distributions admits a **closed form**: its quantile function is simply the (group-weighted)
average of the group-specific quantile functions, so achieving demographic parity reduces to an
inexpensive quantile-based recalibration (Agueh and Carlier, 2011; Chzhen et al., 2020;
Charpentier et al., 2023). The multi-peril case is fundamentally harder. Once the premium is a
*vector*, the one-dimensional quantile machinery no longer applies, no closed-form barycenter
exists in general, and computing the free-support Wasserstein barycenter of the empirical
group distributions requires solving a large linear program — a problem that scales poorly with
portfolio size and with the number of perils.
 
Against this background the thesis makes the following contributions.
 
First, we **formulate** the fair multi-peril pricing problem: we state demographic parity for a
vector of coverage premiums, distinguish it from coordinate-wise (marginal) parity, and cast the
fairness intervention as the projection of each group's joint premium distribution onto their
multivariate Wasserstein barycenter.
 
Second, we give the **theoretical argument** for the barycenter as the correct target — that,
among all demographic-parity-satisfying adjustments, mapping each group to the group-weighted
Wasserstein barycenter is optimal in the sense of minimizing the aggregate transport cost, and
hence the loss of predictive information, incurred in achieving fairness — extending the
one-dimensional characterization to the multivariate, multi-peril setting.
 
Third, because the exact multivariate barycenter is computationally intractable at realistic
scale, we develop a **tractable surrogate** based on the *sliced* Wasserstein barycenter, which
replaces the intractable multivariate optimal-transport problem with an aggregate of
one-dimensional problems along random projections, each of which enjoys the closed-form quantile
solution. This restores computational feasibility while retaining a principled connection to the
exact barycenter.
 
Finally, in keeping with the goal of providing a *framework* rather than a single algorithm, we
situate our proposal among **alternatives** — including entropic (Sinkhorn) regularization of
the exact barycenter, the Gaussian-barycenter special case, and multi-marginal formulations —
and discuss **future directions**, among them the extension to more than two protected groups
and to continuous sensitive attributes, the interaction between joint fairness and the balance
property of the tariff, and the trade-off between marginal and joint parity in multi-peril
products.
 
The remainder of the thesis is organized as follows. Chapter 2 introduces the multiperil pricing problem. Chapter 3 reviews
the necessary background on optimal transport, Wasserstein distances and barycenters. Chapter 4 formalizes the fair regression problem in an insurance context. Chapter 5 develops the transport-based framework its algorithm based on the Sliced Wasserstein barycenter surrogate. Chapter 6 reports numerical experiments on synthetic and real insurance data. Finally, Chapter 7 discusses alternatives, limitations and directions for future work.
